data.title: Balancing The Risk-Reward Tradeoff: Is It Time To Move Toward Cash In Your Financial Portfolio?
data.description:
Portfolio management has to do with the management of an investor's (e.g., an individual's or a family's) financial portfolio over time in terms of risk tolerance, portfolio diversification, investment alternatives, and changing economic conditions.

As a broad starting point, investors may want to consider three or more scenarios of what the future may hold for a broad spectrum of financial assets, i.e., stocks, bonds, and everything else. Famously, Sergio Leone made a spaghetti Western movie called The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly (1966), which starred Clint Eastwood. Along these lines, one simple scenario-based approach might be to think about "best case," "base case," and "worst case" scenarios of what the economic future may hold for investors in the U.S. and around the world.

In this article, I'm interested in exploring a hypothetical worst-case scenario where one assumes that the financial markets do not currently "price in" all the risks facing the U.S. economy, such as inflation, higher unemployment leading to a slowing economy, "stagflation," the economic effects of a prolonged government shutdown and the dismantling of the U.S. government, the risk that the Federal Reserve may lose its independent status, the risk of another cryptocurrency crisis that might lead to a more general financial crisis, and a possible failure of the government to deal with the next financial crisis reasonably. This is Part 1 of a series, as I don't expect to be able to address all of the relevant issues in detail in this article.

The specific question I'd like to focus on is whether investors should "move to cash," i.e., should hold a larger proportion of their financial portfolio in cash or near-cash financial assets. For this article, I will define "cash" as financial assets that are protected by FDIC deposit insurance in the event of a bank failure. Qualified deposits are automatically insured up to at least $250,000 at each FDIC-insured bank. FDIC insurance covers deposit accounts at insured banks, such as checking, savings, money market deposit accounts (MMDAs), and certificates of deposit (CDs). The Securities Investor Protection Corporation (OTCPK:SIPC) provides insurance protection for certain non-deposit investments held in brokerage accounts.

In this article, I aim to explore how changing economic conditions may affect investors' risk tolerance, which would lead to the rebalancing of their investment portfolios. This Seeking Alpha article discusses six issues:

1. Tariffs are a tax on American consumers and businesses and a source of inconvenience, disruptions, and shortages.

Jim Puzzanghera reports that: "Tariffs are collected when foreign goods arrive at the border and are paid by the business bringing them into the country. So they amount to a back-door tax increase on U.S. businesses and consumers, and in particular hit lower-income people harder." The reality is that tariffs raise the prices of food and other imported products and/or cause shortages if importers remove those products from the U.S. market. Annie Lowrey reports that "Households will pay an average of $2,400 more for goods this year, thanks to Trump's policies." It is already evident that tariffs equal taxes and are leading to an affordability crisis in the U.S. SCOTUS may eventually overturn the tariffs based on a "separation of powers" legal argument, but, until they rule, the tariffs will continue to wreak economic havoc for American consumers and businesses.

2. Recession.

Annie Lowrey reports that "Without admitting it, the White House is pursuing a multipronged strategy to raise prices, suppress consumption, freeze production, and lower productivity in the United States." President Donald Trump has been dismantling the U.S. government, but he doesn't seem to understand that he is also dismantling the U.S. economy--once the "egg" is broken, it may not be possible to put it back together. The U.S. economy is based on the government and business working together to efficiently produce the goods and services that American consumers and businesses need. Annie Lowrey reports that Trump's policies "will raise costs and reduce supply." Counterproductive and disruptive economic policies could lead to a recession.

3. Inflation and a recession would mean stagflation.

The reality is that tariffs would likely raise the prices of imported products and/or cause shortages if importers remove those products from the U.S. market. Derek Thompson points out that, "In the 1960s, the politician Iain Macleod coined the term 'stagflation' to describe the UK's combination of faltering growth and surging prices. Like British rock, stagflation crossed the Atlantic and hit the U.S. with hurricane force. Today the term typically refers to our 1970s economy, in which a series of oil crises shut down supply chains, slowed growth, and led to a period of painful inflation." Self-imposed stagflation may result from Trump's economic policies — the U.S. won't be able to blame OPEC for stagflation this time around.

4. Another cybercurrency crisis could be a severe economic shock to the U.S. economy.

Ben McKenzie says that: "Two years ago, we saw a string of banking failures tied to crypto sending shockwaves through the financial system, exposing just how much risk banks had pooled to cater to the industry. While the worst was narrowly averted, the same conditions that sparked that crisis are not only still with us, they’ve metastasized. Thanks to an influx of deregulation and unprecedented political influence, crypto is once again primed to take down the banking system. This time, though, the crash could be even bigger—and we might not be able to bail ourselves out again." Nobel Prize Winner Jean Tirole points out that "Stablecoins arose as a supposed solution to the last of these problems....But financial innovations with an aura of safety often sow the seeds of crises, as derivatives and bundles of subprime securities did before 2008." David Frum reports that "The problem is that the new guardrails protect the profits of issuers without sufficiently reducing the risks to buyers and taxpayers. As a result, the GENIUS Act more likely ensures that the next round of stablecoin failures, when it comes, will be bigger and more destructive to the non-crypto economy." Cybercurrency has no inherent value but just trades on investors' expectations on a moment-by-moment basis. However, stablecoins are different; given the GENIUS Act, one could argue that they are safer for investors than other forms of cybercurrency, with most of their risk transferred to the U.S. financial system as a whole. State Street explains that:

It would be premature to say that stablecoins will precipitate the next financial crisis. Moreover, regulations will surely evolve as the stablecoin industry evolves. Nonetheless, there is sufficient evidence to suggest that mainstreaming stablecoins will stress-test the financial system in the coming years.

5. The Federal Reserve may lose its independence.

Pressure on the Federal Reserve in the Trump era to grow the economy and keep interest rates low presents enormous risks to the U.S. financial system. These risks would be dramatically heightened if the Fed were to lose its independence. Brookings says that "In practice, independence means that the Fed can set interest rates without interference from Congress or the White House, even if politicians are unhappy with Fed policy—and say so publicly." However, the Fed's independence could disappear in the near future. Again, given the GENIUS Act, stablecoins will present enormous challenges for U.S. regulators. Thus, Brookings explains that:

The GENIUS Act makes clear that payment stablecoins are neither a security nor a national currency, nor do they have deposit insurance or automatic access to Federal Reserve payment services. The law says that payment stablecoin issuers can be nonbank entities (federal or state qualified) or subsidiaries of insured depository institutions (IDIs). Issuers’ activities are restricted to offering and redeeming stablecoins and ancillary activities.

This article highlights four key issues for regulators as they implement the GENIUS Act:

First, financial regulators need to write capital, liquidity, and risk management requirements for issuers within 18 months to ensure stable value, and to protect financial stability and the singleness of money (to the maximum extent possible without central bank settlement).

Second, the Treasury, Federal Reserve (Fed), and Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC) need to set conditions under which nonfinancial companies can issue stablecoins to avoid excessive concentration of economic power or increase financial stability risks.

Third, the Treasury needs to make sure that the “comparable regulatory regime” for foreign issuers to offer stablecoins to U.S. residents is as strong as the rules for domestic issuers to reduce arbitrage incentives.

Fourth, FinCEN, a bureau of the Treasury responsible for implementing and enforcing compliance with Anti-Money Laundering/Countering the Financing of Terrorism (AML/CFT) rules, needs to write new regulations to ensure issuers have the technological capacity to counter illicit finance.

6. Unwillingness to "bail out" the economy could result in another Great Depression.

Jon Sindreu says that "During President Trump’s first term, stocks rode high on the belief that he would always pull back on policies that led to a selloff. Now, the administration is making a much tougher pitch: that even if tariffs and budget cuts cause a period of havoc, there are unexpected gains to be made on the other side. The problem is there isn’t much evidence to make investors believe that. Indeed, such views edge close to the “liquidationist” approach historically espoused by laissez-faire economists, and most infamously associated with former President Herbert Hoover’s Treasury secretary [Andrew Mellon] who advised him to let the economy fall."

The lesson of 1929 was that fiscal stimulus was needed to end the Great Depression of the 1930s. The Economist reported that, "But of course, it took the massive, deficit-funded wartime spending of the early 1940s to hammer the final nails into the coffin of the Great Depression. The lesson there, it would seem, is that where stimulus is concerned, massive is better than merely large."

CONCLUSION

Q. Should investors "move to cash," i.e., should investors hold a larger proportion of their financial portfolio in cash or near-cash financial assets?

A. I would say that the answer is yes. There are plenty of reasons why Americans may want to try to make sure that they will weather a severe financial worst-case scenario without too much hardship. The FDIC helps "maintain stability and public confidence in the U.S. financial system. One way we do this is by ensuring deposits of at least $250,000 per depositor, per ownership category, at each FDIC-insured bank."

I want to conclude that investors should do their own due diligence, including consulting with their investment advisors, before they rebalance their investment portfolios. Moving toward a larger proportion of cash or near-cash in investments may tend to reduce investors' investment risk profile, but there could be other factors to consider, including the opportunity cost associated with the possible lost opportunities to grow their investment portfolio over time.

data.title: Saudi Arabia: MBS Visit Reinforces The Thesis' Attractiveness
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying assets that track major Saudi indices such as the iShares MSCI Saudi Arabia (NYSEARCA:KSA) ETF and Franklin FTSE Saudi Arabia (NYSEARCA:FLSA) ETF. Recently, Saudi Arabian Prince Mohammad bin Salman visited President Trump at the White House, and announced major investments in the US.

Saudi Arabia is promoting great diversification of its economy, with a big boost to sectors such as tourism, infrastructure, technology and entertainment. This entire transformation is financed by its barrels of oil, and the most interesting thing is that the P/E of the ETFs that give exposure to this transformation is not yet expensive.

KSA and FLSA

I have ETF articles published recurrently on Seeking Alpha, and have been planning to write about an ETF that tracks Saudi Arabian assets for some time. Why not write in the midst of Mohammad bin Salman 's visit to the White House and President Trump?

The KSA ETF was created to expose investors to Saudi Arabian assets. The ETF currently has $671 million of AuM , and expense ratio of 0.75%. Its performance of -6% year to date also caught my attention, which I see as an opportunity.

The ETF has 169 holdings, and the main three account for more than 30% of the total composition. Although Saudi Arabia is known for its oil sector, this ETF has 40% exposure to financials , 14% to basic material, and almost 12% to energy.

Another option is the FLSA ETF, from the manager Franklin Templeton. In this case, the ETF has only $21 million in AuM, however the expense ratio is only 0.39%, that is, more attractive than the KSA.

Another highlight is that although the Franklin FTSE Saudi Arabia ETF only features 73 holdings, but the weight of the main holdings and the composition of the sectors is very similar to the KSA. This causes tracking error to be low.

In general, the retail investor tends to opt for the FLSA due to the more attractive expense ratio, while a fund manager tends to opt for the KSA due to liquidity.

The Diversification Plan

The starting point is the Vision2030 plan announced in 2016, which aims to diversify the economy and create an environment conducive to investment. The fact is that Saudi Arabia's economy has almost doubled since 2016, as we can see below, and is close to $1.3 trillion.

When Saudi leaders said in their plan that the goal was to diversify the economy, they weren't lying. Non-oil activities will reach half of GDP by 2024. This is a large economic diversification project financed by Saudi oil.

Investment Soared

Foreign investors started by observing, but gradually bought into the idea. In 2025, foreign direct investment grew 24% compared to 2024, and reached $32 billion as we can see below. The detail is that these investments are mostly directed to new sectors, not related to the oil sector.

A great example is the NEOM project, considered the “icing on the cake” of this transformational plan. The project aims to transform the Red Sea coast of northwestern Saudi Arabia into a futuristic city. See below the evolution of the island of Sindalah , one of NEOM's projects, in a photo taken in 2022 and a photo taken in 2024.

There are several projects in this sense, in addition to NEOM, we have The Line, AMAALA, Red Sea Project among others. There are billions of dollars in CapEx adding up all the projects. Another highlight is that, for example, part of the NEOM project is financed by the sovereign wealth fund PIF.

Saudi PIF

The PIF has become gigantic, with projections of reaching $2 trillion in AuM. These are investments in infrastructure, technology, entertainment, football, which give it great influence and power.

In parallel, Saudi Arabia is experiencing a major transformation, such as the female workforce, which has doubled since 2016.

Geopolitical Positioning

Here is one of the great qualities of the Saudi authorities in my opinion. Government officials negotiate with the USA, such as fighter jet purchases and technology agreements, but they also negotiate a lot with China, Israel and Iran.

In a world as marked by geopolitical conflicts as we see today, a country that can deal with such different partners displays a competitive advantage that is difficult for other nations to replicate.

Target Price

To estimate a target price, I will use the P/E multiple with the ETF KSA. As we can see below, in the last 5 years the ETF has traded at lows at 14x earnings and at highs at 21x earnings, making an average of 17.5x earnings.

The ETF currently trades at 15x earnings, so if the ETF returns and trades at the average of the last 5 years, we have an upside of 16.7% which corroborates my recommendation to buy assets that track the main Saudi indices, such as the KSA and FLSA. In my opinion, the risk-return ratio is very attractive due to the exposure to this entire economic transformation.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

As with all investments, we have risks relevant to this thesis. The most serious thing in my opinion is the extremely centralized power in one figure, the prince of Saudi Arabia and prime minister Mohamed bin Salman.

Although models like this can bring rapid economic advancement, there are several controversies regarding markets. An example of these controversies is China, and its control over several markets such as lithium, rare earths, among others.

Last but not least, Seeking Alpha's Quant tools recommend strong selling of the ETF, and this differs from my recommendation to buy due to all the arguments mentioned.

The Bottom Line

The prince of Saudi Arabia recently visited President Trump and announced investments in the United States. However, perhaps an even smarter investment is to buy assets that track Saudi indices, as I have argued in this article.

Saudi Arabia promotes a major transformation in its economy through diversification. Investors refer the country to oil, but the fact is that half of the GDP is already generated by other sectors, such as tourism, infrastructure, technology and entertainment.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the main Saudi indices. In my opinion, the transformation guided by the Vision2030 plan is not currently priced in with ETFs trading at 17.5x earnings. The risk-return ratio seems very attractive.

data.title: Nvidia: Everyone Knows There's A Bubble
data.description:
It seems like everyone is talking about an AI bubble recently, and many high-quality AI stocks have been hammered relentlessly in recent weeks. General market sentiment has turned horrid, as the Fear and Greed Index hit an ultra-low level of six.

Things appear to be so terrible sentiment-wise, that we are approaching the April-lows levels, displaying one of the weakest sentient environments on record. Also, the S&P VIX Index (VIX), Wall Street's Fear Index, has seen a considerable uptick recently.

The VIX Index (Fear Gauge)

The VIX was already elevated, but the sharp post-Nvidia earnings reversal caused the VIX to surge from about 19-28 in a single session. This illustrated nearly a 50% increase in the VIX throughout the session, a reversal of this magnitude is very rare indeed. Also, just look at some of these stats from some of our favorite AI stocks.

Top AI Stocks Down From Their Highs

  • Meta Platforms (META): 28%
  • Oracle (ORCL): 40%
  • Palantir (PLTR): 27%
  • Micron (MU): 25%
  • AMD (AMD): 25%
  • Dell (DELL): 31%
  • Microsoft (MSFT): 15%.

Then, just as it seemed Nvidia (NVDA) had come to the rescue with an excellent earnings announcement, the stock market and AI stocks reversed, in one of the most violent market reversals I've seen in a long time.

Invesco "Nasdaq 100" ETF (QQQ)

QQQ had about a 4% intraday reversal after Nvidia's earnings rally faded, closing around the lows for the session, marking about an 8% pullback from recent highs.

The market has demonstrated that it wasn't done going down yet, no matter how good of a quarter and guidance Nvidia reported. After all, Nvidia reported another excellent quarter but the stock suffered about an 8% reversal from peak to trough.

Nvidia: Another Home Run

If Nvidia's Q3 earnings report isn't a home run, then I don't know what is. The company reported a double beat, topping EPS and revenues estimates. The sales beat of almost $2 billion was significant and illustrated a staggering 62.5% YoY sales gain. Data center revenue increased by 66% YoY.

Perhaps more impressively, the sales outlook for Q4 was huge. The company guided to $65B in revenues vs. the consensus figure of just about $61.84B. GAAP and non-GAAP gross margins are expected to be 74.8% and 75.0%, which is still very high.

With results like these, it's challenging to see what the problem is, given that Nvidia is the leader in the AI space. After all, if things are going really well at Nvidia, the engine essentially powering AI at its base, then surely, this dynamic should be favorable for the broader AI segment, as it sends out positive ripple effects throughout the industry.

So, What Is The Market So Fearful About Now?

Well, it could be several factors, but one of the pressing issues here may be the so called circular financing bubble.

AI circular financing involves a handful of the most prominent tech companies and many of their smaller partner companies to essentially fuel the growth in the AI build out process. Chipmakers like Nvidia, AI model developers like OpenAI (OPENAI), massive cloud providers like Oracle, and other major tech firms invest billions in each other. This phenomenon has a "loop" effect as it drives buying of AI hardware, software, and services from one another on a massive scale.

Yet, some pressing questions exist, like how much AI investment is too much? Will these investments prove economically viable in the end? Also, what about the massive debt many of these companies are taking on? What will happen once the spending slows down, and when will this bubble ultimately burst?

In my view, while we're seeing some deflation in AI and momentum stocks in general, it is unlikely the end of the AI super cycle, yet. Instead, I believe we are experiencing a healthy and necessary correction process.

Also, there could be much more AI investment and M&A as the easier monetary phase potentially kicks into high gear next year, and possibly in 2027. At the same time we must be mindful that at some point the AI-bull market will end, and we could witness a significant crash then.

Another Thing Troubling Markets

The AI circular trade may be a longer term issue, but the market may be more nervous because of a more hawkish Fed now. Bitcoin (BTC-USD) crashed to about $80K recently, which is about a 35% decline from the recent highs. Bitcoin losing so much ground illustrates liquidity outflows from the ultimate risk asset market, which is spilling over into equity markets, and it's likely because of the overly hawkish Fed.

No Cut In December?

I have said that Fed Chair Jerome Powell's hawkish tone at the last FOMC meeting would be a problem. Earlier, the market was much more skeptical about a no-cut possibility in December, but now there is about a 2/3 probability that there will not be a rate cut in December.

This dynamic complicates matters in the near term, putting more pressure and creating increased uncertainty in markets, also putting the holiday/year-end rally into question.

Still, I wonder, have the rate cut probabilities tilted too bearish, and in a worst-case scenario, we may miss one rate cut, but, on the bight side, we have a much easier monetary environment to look forward to in 2026.

Wall Street Remains Bullish on Nvidia

Despite the recent volatility in Nvidia's stock, the average price target on the street is close to $250. Moreover, given the recent stellar earnings and guidance, it seems likely that price target estimates could get lifted more in future months. Also, the higher end estimates range to over $350, implying that the stock could have 40-100% upside in the next 12 months.

On the downside, the lowest estimates are around $140-150, which could also get lifted after the recent earnings announcement. This dynamic suggests that the downside may be limited in Nvidia's stock, and the risk/reward ratio is firmly skewed to the upside here.

The Bottom Line: Is Nvidia In A Bubble?

Nvidia's current forward P/E ratio is about 25, which is not terribly expensive, if the company can maintain a solid growth trajectory in the coming years. Moreover, Nvidia's forward EPS growth could average about 33% over the next several years. Therefore, Nvidia trades at a forward PEG ratio of only about 0.75 here.

A 0.75 PEG is cheap for a value company. However, given that Nvidia is still a growth company, it could have a PEG ratio of about 1.5 and still be considered relatively inexpensive. Thus, the market may be overly pessimistic regarding Nvidia and other top AI companies here. It seems probable that once the AI scare phase passes and we receive more clarity regarding future Fed policy, the market may return to driving valuations and multiples higher.

Therefore, I don't see a reason why Nvidia's stock (and other top tech stocks) couldn't increase by 50-100% over the next 12-18 months or so under the right circumstances, where we have a more accessible monitor environment coupled with continued AI growth. Due to this dynamic we are likely seeing and approaching many high quality intermediate and longer-term buying opportunities in the market now.

data.title: Elastic: Don't Overlook This Name As Software M&A Picks Up Steam
data.description:
Amid the resounding bearishness that is upending the AI trade, investors should look to portfolio rotation to protect themselves from possible further downside in the S&P 500. In my view, the best way to achieve this is to emphasize “growth at a reasonable price” in our portfolio.

Mid-cap software stocks have been in a tough spot this year as fears of AI disruption have weakened the group, but it has also created tremendous buying opportunities in many hollowed-out names. Elastic (ESTC), an enterprise search company, looks particularly appealing. The company just reported a beat-and-raise in Q3, and despite this, the stock fell more than 10%, bringing Elastic’s year-to-date losses to near 30%.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Elastic in September, when the stock was hovering near $85 per share. Since then, Elastic has boosted its full-year guidance outlook, supported by healthy consumption and net revenue retention rates, while also implementing a new buyback plan to take advantage of falling share prices. I’m reiterating my "Buy" rating on Elastic.

Since the value component is such a big portion of Elastic’s draw, let’s first start with a discussion of the stock’s latest multiples against its raised guidance. At current post-earnings share prices near $73, Elastic trades at a market cap of $7.71 billion. After we net off the $1.40 billion of cash and $570.3 million of debt on Elastic’s latest balance sheet, its enterprise value goes to $6.88 billion.

For FY26 (the year for Elastic ending in April 2026), the company has revised its guidance upward to $1.715-$1.721 billion, which reflects 15% constant currency growth at the midpoint, a 2-point bump versus 13% growth previously.

And for FY27, Street expectations rise 14% y/y to $1.94 billion. This positions Elastic’s valuation multiples at:

  • 4.0x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 3.5x EV/FY27 revenue

We have to recognize that Elastic is a software business that is solidly balancing growth and profitability (it’s not exactly at the Rule of 40, but easily exceeds the Rule of 30), is adapting to the AI age by introducing an AI search-like product that feeds companies’ core need to index and query large data pools for inference, and is making use of its substantial balance sheet liquidity to buy back stock. The company has over $300 million open on its buyback authorization, worth roughly 5% of the company’s current market cap.

We note that while the whole software sector has seen a correction this year (except for the stocks that the market has deemed AI winners, like Oracle (ORCL) and Microsoft (MSFT)), few stocks in Elastic’s growth bucket have sunk to such low multiples. As shown below, peers like HubSpot (HUBS) and Atlassian (TEAM) that are also growing in the high teens/~20% range tend to trade at a mid/high single-digit multiple.

One more point is well worth mentioning. Though the market may have dumped a lot of small/mid-cap software stocks this year, corporate acquirers have been filling the void. Adobe (ADBE) just bought SEO specialist Semrush for a 70% premium; a few months back, private equity giant Thoma Bravo acquired Dayforce. While hoping for an M&A takeout should never be the sole reason to invest in any stock, we should take comfort in the fact that a low valuation should indicate there may be window-shoppers here.

Take advantage of these low prices as a buying opportunity.

Q2 download

Let’s now go through Elastic’s latest fiscal Q2 (September quarter) results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Elastic’s revenue of $423.5 million grew 16% y/y, beating Street expectations of $418.3 million (+15% y/y) by a one-point margin. We note that growth was helped by one point of FX (with the weakening dollar leading to positive translation impacts), but we do also recognize that revenue growth softened from 18% y/y in Q2.

That said, we are also encouraged by the fact that the company’s remaining performance obligations (RPO), or essentially backlog, also grew at 17% y/y (15% constant currency), which makes it unlikely for Elastic’s revenue growth to decelerate much further. Also, we note that Elastic maintained a TTM net expansion rate of 112%, indicating that the average customer is boosting their spending on Elastic by 12%. Many software companies have struggled to maintain healthy expansion rates this year amid headcount pressure on seat-based products: Elastic is spared this headwind.

The company continues to note success with pushing for higher-value deals among upmarket enterprise customers. Per CEO Ash Kulkarni's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

In Q2, we secured significant customer commitments across all solution areas. We maintained strong momentum in search and AI while also seeing an uptick in platform consolidation for security and observability with an increasing number of customers migrating from legacy products to our platform.

These factors led to an acceleration in the number of large deals we were able to secure this quarter. In Q2, we signed over 30 commitments valued over $1 million in annual commitment value with 5 representing over $10 million in total contract value. Of these 5 deals, 2 were greater than $20 million, a new record this quarter. In Q1 2025, we strategically realigned our sales team to focus capacity on our highest value opportunities. This quarter marked the fifth consecutive quarter of disciplined sales execution, demonstrating our continued commitment to driving enhanced performance and consistency across the field.

That said, Elastic certainly didn't print a perfect quarter. We note that investments into sales capacity to secure these large deals yielded a 20% y/y increase in sales and marketing expense, faster than revenue. This led to slight deleverage in pro forma operating margins, down 110bps y/y to 16.5%.

That said, we note that these large multi-year contracts should have a long tail of revenue contribution, with sales investments made upfront, allowing for economies of scale as more of these contracts are recognized as revenue. The company's guidance for Q3 calls for operating margins to improve to 17.5%.

Key takeaways

In my view, Elastic's >10% post-earnings correction is not at all commensurate with the high quality of its earnings results. While we are keeping an eye on slight growth deceleration and a lack of margin expansion, we remain encouraged by healthy net expansion rates, large deals in the pipeline, and a very undemanding valuation. Stay long here.

data.title: Matthews International: Excellent Deal On Warehouse Automation Sale
data.description:
Matthews International (MATW) sold its warehouse automation business at an attractive price with respect to our models, showing that the board is significantly changing its tact with respect to portfolio exits which was the intention of the activists in the business to support a deleveraging and some portfolio rotation as discussed in our previous coverage. Next year, we should be getting another meeting which will give yet another chance to the activists to take control of the board with the contest last time being quite tight, with the proxy advisory companies in favour of the activist appointees - though it was not enough for the activists to succeed in their bid to take over the board. The other positive news is what seems to be an end to the legal spat with Tesla (TSLA) that took down MATW stock a notch and wasn't recovered from.

Latest

On the sale of the warehouse automation business. The consideration for the business is only around 10% short of the EV we had for the whole business based on a 10x EV/EBITDA multiple at $230 million.

There is a lot left in the industrial tech business. Firstly there is the soon to be released industrial printing solution which will also have a recurring component from the consumables. Then of course there is also the DBE battery manufacturing equipment business which was the subject of an attempted injunction by Tesla on the basis that Matthews had stolen their battery technology. The courts ruled in Matthews' favour for the initial ruling and then also for the attempt to appeal the ruling it would seem ending the battle for good, and removing this legal cloud from the stock. With possibly half the EBITDA remaining in the segment in business areas that would be considered more exciting than warehouse automation, which was an area that the activists were pushing for an exit from much like the SGK business, we think value is being highlighted with the residual EV for industrial tech being much less than half (10% of the EV for the residual businesses as per our model).

Latest Quarter

However, together with both the strong SGK sale and now the warehouse automation sale, Matthews hasn't really re-entered higher ground. We believe further catalysts could come from a successful move by the activists in controlling the board, even though current management seems to be falling in line without the need for an activist board. Last quarter (Q3) had $180 million in annualised EBITDA and $682 million in net debt, so a 3.7x leverage multiple. Deleveraging is an important capital allocation objective.

With the sale of the warehouse automation business to Duravant, and taking away around $72 million in sales at the segment EBITDA margin of 10%, leverage falls to a much more manageable 2.5x. This is the target, and could allow for other capital allocation priorities to take precedence.

In terms of the value case, the idea continues to be that the memorialisation business is where most of the value in the business lies, and this is even more our feeling as private markets finally get up to speed again opening up a market of financial sponsors who may be interested in the sorts of cash cow businesses that have debt capacity in an environment where costs of capital are finally falling. As mentioned in our previous coverage, demographic growth should resume trends in death rates to support memorialisation and declines in some materials like lumber and steel should continue to leak into gross margin for the business though with some lag - though other materials like brass and copper are seeing higher prices.

We have been seeing a pickup in the memorialisation EBITDA but the reason is pricing push. Materials costs have gone up on average, and that's despite commodity deflation and the effect of the materials costing lag due to how long it takes for them to go through material inventory. Cost savings are also bearing fruit though and in the end margins are up. But it's also the YoY benefit of the UK disposal.

Industrial tech is still challenged in DBE due to the litigation with Tesla but that is going to be in the rear view hopefully soon enough, with their efforts to court interest with their demonstration line hopefully going to bear fruit. Sales reductions meant EBITDA margin declines despite cost saving efforts.

In terms of SGK, the contribution remains from the disposed business within the reporting here but on a 3 month lag and in the proportion of their ownership (40%).

The leverage ratio hasn't fallen. This is really quite annoying considering the disposals were happening at great multiples, and this was at the core of the value idea here. Cash draw is still occurring from "strategic initiatives", almost a third of which this quarter is legal costs. Relative to the $350 million consideration for the sale from SGK, almost $22 million went just into legal costs. Total strategic charges are $33.9 million. Operating cash flow was less than -$20 million, and then there was also the $56 million acquisition of the Dodge Company presumably to bolster memorialisation with a bolt-on, and the proceeds from SGK were dampened by around $100 million from total investing outflows. That would have been an 18% deleveraging. With the legal costs in the rear view soon, and with no more announced acquisitions, we should expect to see a clear deleveraging with the warehouse automation business.

Thanks to the UK disposal and business simplification, gross margins are up, and so are EBITDA margins (adjusted). Significant interest line at around $14 million per quarter against $58 million EBITDA should also abate with lower capital costs in the US and with another round of debt reductions and deleveraging.

Bottom Line

We should see a deleveraging coming soon after the proceeds are in from the warehouse automation sale, memorialisation seems to have enough pricing power, cost of capital declines should support more likely portfolio exits to sponsors, and more activist heat should be coming in a couple of months. Reconciliations including big legal expenses should also start getting simpler to support more cash-accurate adjusted EBITDA figures. With catalysts approaching and the business case unfolding we think it's a good time for Matthews. Relatively recession resistant as well in its businesses that have not already copped big hits.

data.title: Why The Market Fell So Hard: 3 Reasons
data.description:
The U.S. market experienced a rare event: the S&P 500 (SPY) opened up 1.4% yesterday but closed down 1.56%. This specific pattern has occurred only twice over the last five years: in April 2020, right after the COVID crash, and in April 2025 following a major selloff. Both times it signaled serious market instability. Now the pattern has repeated for the third time, and I see several reasons why it could happen.

Reasons For A Selloff

Nvidia

The first reason and the most telling one was the reaction to Nvidia's (NVDA) earnings. The company beat profit expectations and raised guidance, yet the stock fell 7%, erasing all the gains. Nvidia dragged down the entire technology sector because if the market leader can't hold gains on positive news, it's hard to do the same for anyone else.

Even though I remain bullish on Nvidia and see the results as excellent, it's hard to ignore the fact that the market's opinion on the valuation of AI-related companies is volatile. According to Goldman Sachs, since November 2022, the S&P 500 has added $23 trillion in market capitalization, while Goldman Sachs' technology basket of private companies and hyperscalers gained $19T. However, Goldman's estimates of the present discounted value of potential AI capital revenues tell a different story. Even in the upper-bound scenario, AI revenues justify only $19T of value creation, roughly matching what tech companies alone have already captured. The baseline estimate sits at $8T, while the lower bound is just $5T dollars.

Semiconductors and private companies combined have added $8-9T in market cap, already exceeding the baseline projection and approaching the upper bound. This means the market has essentially priced in Goldman's most optimistic AI revenue scenario across the entire S&P 500, leaving no margin for disappointment. If actual AI monetization tracks closer to baseline or lower bound estimates, significant multiple compression may become inevitable in the longer term, regardless of whether companies meet their earnings targets. Again, any valuation estimates should be taken with a grain of salt, given the unpredictable nature of such fundamental innovation as artificial intelligence.

Fed Policy

The second reason is uncertainty about Fed policy. September employment data came in fine, but the data didn't clarify whether the Federal Reserve will cut rates in December. The probability of a December cut rose to 41%, indicating that market participants are still mostly unsure that the cut will happen.

CTA Funds

The third reason comes from systematic CTA funds that trade based on trends. GS models show CTA funds may sell $41.8B dollars worth of assets over the next month at current S&P 500 levels, though if the market drops further, sales may increase up to 66 bn dollars. The most serious risk, according to Goldman Sachs, is the index falling below a key technical level at 6456.

Then, CTA funds may start selling the bulk of their positions, amplifying the decline. Right now, the S&P 500 Index (SP500) trades around 6570, leaving little margin of safety for traders.

Time To Buy The Dip?

The key question now is whether the S&P 500 breaks below 6456, where an avalanche of automatic selling from systematic traders may start. However, if the S&P 500 consolidates above this level, then a year-end rally will be quite probable. Thus, the next few days may determine whether we see another 5-8% correction or a steady market recovery until the end of the year.

At the same time, the Fear & Greed index shows extreme fear in markets, while I don't see a single fundamental reason for such a harsh sell-off, at least in the short-term.

The U.S. economy is still not in a recession, and the U.S. GDP may grow more than 4% in Q3 2025. Big US tech earnings are strong, Walmart's earnings are fine too (which I see as a proxy indicator for U.S. consumers' health), and the Fed will continue cutting rates anyway in the foreseeable future. Therefore, I think gradual buying of the dip makes sense in the current market context, though more risk-averse investors may wait when the S&P 500 moves far enough away from the key technical level of 6456.

data.title: Hiring Rebounds, But Fed Path Still Looks Foggy Ahead Of December 10 Rate Decision
data.description:
By James Picerno

US jobs rose in September, increasing more than expected, according to the shutdown-delayed report from the Labor Department. The increase strengthens the view that the third-quarter GDP report, when it’s finally published, will reflect robust economic activity.

Looking deeper into the jobs report, however, continues to raise questions about what’s in store for the months ahead. Although hiring recovered to the strongest gain in five months, the year-over-year trend continues to weaken, slipping to a 0.9% advance in September - the softest since the pandemic was raging. In addition, August payrolls were revised to a loss of 4,000 jobs, while July’s gain was adjusted down to 72,000, leaving employment for the two months a combined 33,000 lower than earlier reported. The unemployment rate, which is tabulated separately from the payroll numbers, ticked higher to 4.4% - the highest in four years.

The debate about the economy’s near-term outlook - and the implications for interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve - remains unsettled. Both sides can point to numbers that favor, or reject, a case for a third rate cut this year at next month’s policy meeting, but a consensus at the central bank looks increasingly elusive.

Fed funds futures in recent days have been volatile, shifting from a coin toss on the odds for more easing at the December 10 policy to a modest edge for predicting no change in rates later in the week. The latest update shows the futures market again showing favorable odds for a cut.

One of the few points of clarity is that a US recession still looks like a low probability for the near term, based the TMC Recession Probability Indicator (RPI), which aggregates data from three business cycle indicators published by regional Fed banks (see here for details). The current reading (via numbers through November 1) implies a roughly 5% likelihood that the economy is contracting.

A closer look at daily numbers for RPI reminds that while recession risk still appears low, it’s been edging higher lately, which suggests that headwinds could be building for early 2026. Meantime, short of a macro shock, the indicator appears unlikely to signal a recession warning in the immediate future.

Supporting RPI’s low recession risk estimate is the latest nowcast for the delayed third-quarter GDP report, which is expected to accelerate to a strong 4.2% seasonally adjusted annual rate, according to the Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow model (as of November 19) - up from Q2’s robust 3.8% advance.

The question is whether the momentum carries over into the fourth quarter. Answering will be tougher than usual now that the Labor Department canceled the October jobs report, citing the government shutdown, which prevented the agency from collecting data last month.

The data void arrives at a time when divisions within the central bank have deepened on opinions for whether it’s timely to cut rates again next month.

“In discussing the near-term course of monetary policy, participants expressed strongly differing views about what policy decision would most likely be appropriate at the committee’s December meeting,” Fed minutes said for the October 28-29 policy meeting.

Chicago Federal Reserve president Austan Goolsbee, who’ll cast a vote at the December 10 policy meeting, is on the fence on how to proceed. “I’m not decided going into the December meeting,” he said this week, before the September payrolls report was released. “I am nervous about the inflation side of the ledger, where you’ve seen inflation above the target for 4.5 years and it’s trending the wrong way.”

Fed Governor Waller, by contrast, supports a rate cut. Speaking at a conference on Monday, he said: “I am not worried about inflation accelerating or inflation expectations rising significantly. My focus is on the labor market, and after months of weakening, it is unlikely that the September jobs report later this week or any other data in the next few weeks would change my view that another cut is in order.”

Normally, these disagreements would move closer to a consensus view, as incoming data is published ahead of the rate decision. But thanks to the government shutdown and other factors, these are far from “normal” times.

“What do you do if you’re driving in the fog?” asked Fed Chair Powell late last month. “You slow down.”

Three weeks later, the analogy remains timely. Leaving rates unchanged, as a result, remains a reasonable policy choice until the data fog lifts.

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company

data.title: AMD Sees Growth Tailwinds With Inferencing (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) is gaining traction for its Data Center CPUs & GPUs, striking a 6GW deal with OpenAI (OPENAI) in Q3 ’25 that is expected to drive substantial revenue growth in the coming years. With AMD gaining significant market share in Client Compute, I believe the organization will be well positioned to realize margin-accretive growth in the coming quarters. Adding to the mix, I believe the hyperscalers will be going through an ongoing GPU refresh cycle beginning in the next year, compounding new capacity growth with replacement sales. Given the robust outlook, I am recommending AMD shares with a Buy rating with a price target of $286/share at 7.57x eFY27 price/sales.

You can review my previous coverage of AMD here:

“AMD Reshapes Client And Gaming, Taking Over Market Share.”

Advanced Micro Devices Operations

In Q3 ’25, AMD realized strong growth in its Data Center market segment driven by the ramp-up of MI350 GPUs as well as the Gen-5 EPYC Turin CPUs. CPU sales were largely driven by deployment across the hyperscalers for both 1st party services and cloud services. Setting the stage for 2025, AMD confirmed the launch of EPYC Venice CPUs in 2026, the next iteration of the EPYC series CPU chips built on 2nm process technology.

Expanding on its technology stack, AMD released ROCm 7 in q3’25, providing 4.6x higher inference & performance and 3x higher training performance when compared to the previous generation. ROCm 7 was designed to support AMD’s MI350X and MI355X GPUs. With this release, AMD is narrowing the performance gap with Nvidia’s (NVDA) CUDA software, though it can provide users with a cost advantage in the range of 15-40%, depending on performance.

Bringing it all together will be AMD’s Helios rack-scale solution, integrating MI400 GPUs, Venice EPYC CPUs, and Pensando NICs to optimize performance, power, cooling, and serviceability. In q3’25, AMD closed the sale of the ZT manufacturing business to Sanmina, which will remain a critical partner for manufacturing the Helios rack-scale solution.

One of the value drivers that AMD may have over Nvidia is developing both the CPU & GPU and rack-scale integration. Though this may provide AMD an edge in the near-term, Nvidia is positioning itself to retain its leadership in AI acceleration for inferencing, partnering with Intel Corporation (INTC) to develop a series of custom x86 CPUs to integrate into the NVLink ecosystem to create a fully integrated rack-scale AI supercomputer.

On October 6, 2025, AMD announced a partnership agreement with OpenAI to deploy 6GW of compute capacity with the first 1GW deployment expected to begin in e2h26. As part of the broader partnerships, I believe Oracle Corp. (ORCL) will be responsible for outfitting the data centers with AMD’s hardware. As part of the agreement, AMD issued a warrant for upwards of 160mm shares of AMD common stock to OpenAI, structured to vest as milestones are reached.

The broader hyperscaler theme can provide substantial tailwinds for AMD in the coming years as the hyperscalers gear their capital outlays towards building out compute capacity. In total, the hyperscalers have a collective backlog of $1.21T that is largely tied to developing AI infrastructure.

As a result, I’m expecting a substantial increase in capital spend by these companies to convert backlog into revenue, creating substantial opportunity for growth amongst the AI accelerator companies like AMD, Nvidia, and Broadcom (AVG). With the recently announced partnership with OpenAI, AMD may gain some additional market share despite Nvidia’s dominance in the GPU space. I believe this will be the result of the transition into further adoption of AI inferencing as well as the need for a diversified technology portfolio to better service customers operating on the ROCm software stack.

Another factor investors should consider is the refresh cycle for AI infrastructure. Cloud-hosted GPUs have an expected life of 1-3 years, suggesting relatively high turnover for the processor. This may result in a compounding growth effect for AMD in which the early iterations may need to be replaced in the coming year. This may mean that the hyperscalers will not only be expanding capacity but also replacing aging capacity. Given the ongoing ramp-up of AI compute capacity, the refresh cycle may be ongoing, meaning that GPU sales may compound over the years rather than just realize new capacity sales.

In Client & Gaming, AMD realized strength across the portfolio with revenue growing by 73% when compared to the previous year’s quarter. In addition to this, the market segment realized an improved operating margin, expanding from 12% to 21% during this period. Growth for the segment was largely driven by Ryzen-powered notebook sales as well as enterprise adoption of Ryzen PCs. As for gaming, AMD realized strong tailwinds as Sony (SONY) and Microsoft (MSFT) ramped up production for the upcoming holiday season.

For semi-custom chips, AMD closed a number of design wins in aerospace & defense, automotive, data center, and communications, all valued at $45b; revenue for this market is expected to begin ramping up in 2026.

On November 19, 2025, White House officials “urged members of Congress to oppose the GAIN AI Act,” which would impact Nvidia and AMD’s ability to sell AI chips to China while prioritizing domestic sales. In contrast, Amazon (AMZN) and Microsoft (MSFT) have voiced support of the GAIN AI Act given that the Act will prioritize sales to domestic companies over China, particularly given the tight supply for the advanced AI accelerators. From a trade standpoint, the GAIN AI Act could significantly impede President Trump’s negotiations with China in relation to sourcing rare earth elements and magnets, critical factors necessary for developing motors used in electric vehicles, robotics, as well as guiding systems and radar for defense systems. If the Act were to pass, AMD’s business in China may be significantly impacted, particularly given the high demand for compute capacity amongst the cloud services providers.

Advanced Micro Devices Financial Position

Looking ahead to Q4 ’25, I’m forecasting AMD to generate $10.13b in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $1.33/share. I’m expecting that revenue growth will largely be driven by growth in the Data Center and Client & Gaming market segments, particularly through its CPU chips. In the quarter, I’m expecting the Data Center operating margin to remain relatively soft, particularly as AMD ramps up production of the next generation Instinct GPUs. Margins for Client & Gaming should continue to expand in the quarter driven by scaled growth.

AMD’s balance sheet remains healthy with $7.24b in cash & equivalent and $3.22b in debt for a net cash position of $4b. AMD experienced some build in working capital that is more or less in line with seasonal trends, maintaining a stable inventory level in Q3 ’25.

Risks Related to Advanced Micro Devices

Bull Case

AMD is expanding its market share for Client & Gaming, effectively executing in consumer and enterprise PC shipments. Though Data Center remains relatively soft with respect to Nvidia’s growth, AMD’s partnership agreement with OpenAI may provide substantial tailwinds for the coming years, driving stronger growth for the chip designer.

Bear Case

AMD’s Data Center segment trails Nvidia’s market share by a wide margin; with Nvidia’s recently announced partnership with Intel, Nvidia may retain its dominance in accelerated computing, particularly for inferencing.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

AMD shares are currently trading at 11.45x price/sales, a relatively expensive premium considering the through-cycle. From a tactical perspective, AMD shares appear to be going through a downcycle, though this may be reversed following Nvidia’s upbeat Q3 ’26 earnings results.

Given the long-cycled approach to AMD’s sales growth trajectory, I believe modeling a price target based on eFY27 sales will be most appropriate, providing flexibility for its deal with OpenAI to materialize. Given this factor, I am recommending AMD shares with a Buy rating with a price target of $286/share at 7.57x eFY27 price/sales.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: BLOX: Crypto Miners And Cryptocurrencies In One ETF
data.description:
Thesis

The crypto space is a fairly recent one, and more importantly it is very new when it comes to traditional capital markets instruments. It took a long time for crypto to become available via exchange traded funds, and the respective universe has further expanded with the advent of ancillary businesses such as crypto miners.

Capital markets waste no time, and traditional ETF providers have flooded the market with exchange traded products which offer investors access to these segments. In today's article we are going to talk about the Nicholas Crypto Income ETF (BLOX) a new addition to the exchange traded universe.

Several types of crypto exposure

We are going to start by offering investors a quick overview of what is present in the market with respect to crypto:

1. Outright exposure to a cryptocurrency via an ETF

We have summarized them by cryptocurrency, the holdings (spot or futures) and also provided readers with the tickers for the leveraged 2x ETFs for each name. We have covered in the past IBIT, BITO and ETHT on the Seeking Alpha platform.

Kindly note that we are not making any judgements regarding the asset class or crypto in general, but merely listing the largest regulated funds in the space and their leveraged equivalents.

2. Bitcoin miners and infrastructure ETFs

The second category has to do with ETFs which aggregate bitcoin miners, infrastructure providers and crypto trading platforms. In this category we can find funds like the Bitwise Crypto Industry Innovators ETF (BITQ), or the VanEck Digital Transformation ETF (DAPP). These names target exchange listed equities that are part of the crypto ecosystem, but do not contain cryptocurrencies outright.

3. A blend of cryptocurrencies and bitcoin miners/infrastructure companies

BLOX falls in the third category, and represents a portfolio which is a blend of outright cryptocurrencies and miners/infrastructure companies.

What does the fund do and what does the fund hold?

As stated above, BLOX blends outright cryptocurrencies with companies which operate in the larger crypto ecosystem:

The fund holds roughly 30% in bitcoin via the VanEck Bitcoin ETF (HODL) and the Fidelity Origin Bitcoin Fund (FBTC), and another 10% in ether via the iShares Ethereum Trust (ETHA). So that is a rough 40% outright holding in cryptocurrencies.

Crypto miners represent approximately 30% of the holdings, via names such as Mara Holdings (MARA) and Galaxy Digital (GLXY). Trading platforms represent another 15% via names such as Coinbase (COIN) and Robinhood (HOOD), while semiconductor stocks are another 10%. The residual is held in cash.

So as a summary, we have the following segmentation:

  • cryptocurrencies: 40%
  • miners: 30%
  • trading platforms: 15%
  • semiconductor stocks 10%

What is very particular about this ETF though is the fact that it does not simply hold equities outright, but also aims to extract dividends via options strategies:

The fund seeks to generate income primarily using options contracts on some or all of the individual holdings of the equity and crypto portfolio. BLOX portfolio managers will select options strategies that it believes will best provide the Fund with current income while also attempting to capture upside appreciation.

BLOX has the additional feature of offering dividends paid weekly out of its holdings. This is not a bad strategy for a very pro-cyclical and volatile asset class. We are not going to go through the options structures because they represent complex ways of doing a very simple thing - extracting dividends from a portfolio of equities. The fund's current distribution rate is 36%, but like any fund that employs such a strategy, the distribution rate is meaningless in isolation, and what we should look at is total return.

Performance so far

The fund is fairly new, having come to market in June 2025. We shall now compare it with a number of the above mentioned ETFs:

Although BLOX is down an astounding -40% since early October 2025, the fund is still positive on a 6-month basis when using a total return measurement tool. Please note the close correlation with BITQ as per the graph above.

BLOX is a great tool to use when looking for a very high beta crypto industry investment that will also extract dividends for you from the underlying holdings. We find this name to be a good building block to use at the beginning or during the intermediate phases of a recovery. An investor here does not have to choose an exit point, with the fund constantly harvesting gains.

Potential for a rebound

The crypto ecosystem has been in a massive downturn in the past month, with eye-popping drops:

In the past month we have seen drops in the mid 20s percentage-wise for crypto related names, with some outliers like MARA down -43%. BLOX is in the middle of the cohort with a -22% move (again we are using a total return calculation here).

While the long term performance for the fund is dependent on where we are in the crypto cycle, short term the market is oversold when looking at RSI levels and sentiment:

The RSI for the ETF is currently 28, sub the 30 threshold for oversold conditions:

An RSI below 30 is considered an oversold condition, which suggests a potential buy signal. This indicates the asset may be undervalued and due for a rebound.

Oversold markets tend to bounce, even if for short periods of time. On a longer time-frame (12 months and more) an investor needs to make an educated decision if we are in the middle of a crypto cycle or towards the end. On a shorter time frame though we can also see the name touching the lower end of its Bollinger Band, which again in many instances is the sign of a short term relief rally to correct oversold conditions. Nothing goes lower in a straight line in finance, and extreme technical levels like today's need to mend via either a stabilization or relief rally before another potential leg down.

We like BLOX for what it tries to do, and we find it to be an apt tool to speculate the crypto boom during a recovery phase, but it is unclear where we stand with the crypto macro currently. We are looking for a shorter term bounce here in BLOX because of oversold conditions, but long term prospects are macro dependent.

Conclusion

BLOX is a new exchange traded fund. The name blends outright cryptocurrencies holdings with crypto miners and trading platforms. The ETF further structures its portfolio with options in order to constantly extract dividends from the portfolio. We like BLOX as a tool during a market recovery, but its performance is very much dependent on the macro crypto cycle.

Yes, an RSI below 30 is considered an oversold condition, which suggests a potential buy signal. This indicates the asset may be undervalued and due for a rebound, but it's crucial to use this as a starting point and not as a sole indicator for making investment decisions.

data.title: Nvidia Looks Undervalued
data.description:
Introduction

Per my May article, Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) is better prepared for the future than competitors. The fiscal Q2 '26 and Q3 '26 numbers have come out since then, and they continue to show the benefits Nvidia sees from their preparations.

Nvidia quarters are one year and one month off from calendar quarters. For example, their fiscal Q3 '26 period ended October 26, 2025, but I treat this as calendar Q3 '25 when I compare it with other companies.

My thesis is that Nvidia looks undervalued for long-term investors who plan to own it for at least 3 years.

Using AI Internally

Looking at the last few 10-Ks, we see Nvidia has been hiring more employees. They jumped from 29,600 employees in January 2024 up to 36,000 in January 2025. Additionally, each human employee is working with a $10,000 AI to increase productivity. Many headlines talk about AI taking existing jobs and shrinking the workforce. This is not what we see at Nvidia. Rather, AI is reducing the rate at which they need to hire new employees. Without AI, the pace and scale of new hardware designs would be limited. It would be impossible for them to design new systems in a timely manner without the use of internal AI.

CEO Jensen Huang explained the way Nvidia is augmenting employees with AI agents during a September 2025 BG2 podcast with analyst Brad Gerstner:

Suppose I were to hire a $100,000 employee. And I augmented that $100,000 employee with a $10,000 AI. And that $10,000 AI as a result made that $100,000 employee twice more productive, three times more productive. Would I do it? Heartbeat. I'm doing it across every single person in our company right now.

Using AI internally, Nvidia is increasing revenue per employee like no other mature company in the space ($ in millions):

Calendar

Year

Nvidia

AMD

Intel

TSMC

Google

Amazon

2022

$1.0

$0.9

$0.5

$0.4

*2025

$5.0

*The calendar 2025 estimate of $5 million in annual revenue per employee for Nvidia above is based on management’s guidance of revenue of $65 billion for next quarter plus the $147.8 billion in revenue from the last 3 quarters for an estimated calendar ‘25 revenue sum of $212.8 billion. They increased employees from 6,400 when they went from 29,600 for calendar ‘23 to 36,000 for calendar ‘24. If they do this again for calendar ‘25 then they will have about 42,400 employees. We get to a revenue per employee estimate of $5 million for calendar ‘25 when dividing this revenue estimate by this employee headcount estimate.

There are other companies out there that are rapidly increasing the revenue per employee, but these are often new companies that don’t have positive operating income and positive free cash flow (“FCF”) like Nvidia.

In the same podcast mentioned above, CEO Huang explains how Nvidia's use of AI internally powers their growth. Eventually other companies will be able to have similar stories, but Nvidia is far ahead right now:

Every single software engineer, every single chip designer in our company already has AIs working with them. 100% coverage. As a result, the number of chips we're building is better. The number is growing. The pace at which we're doing it is, right? And so we're growing faster as a company. As a result, we're hiring more people. Our productivity is greater. Our top line's greater. Our profitability is greater. What's not to love about that? Now apply the Nvidia story to the world's GDP.

Semiconductor Revenue And Competition

Looking at the semiconductor landscape since calendar Q1 '23, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) has grown nicely, but Nvidia’s growth is unmatched:

*The calendar Q4 '25 numbers above are from guidance.

Based on the chart above, it is clear Nvidia is growing in ways Intel (INTC) and AMD are not. These competitors have not yet slowed Nvidia down as the AI pie keeps growing.

Alphabet/Google (GOOG, GOOGL) has been a competitor for years with their TPU architecture, which is now on TPU7 Ironwood. It is only Nvidia and Google that go back many years in terms of providing a serious volume of AI hardware. Amazon (AMZN) has only emerged recently as a serious competitor with their Trainium architecture. Nvidia is in every cloud, but this isn’t the case for Google and Amazon. Unfortunately, Google does not break out revenue for TPUs, and Amazon does not break out revenue for Trainium, so they could not be in the chart above.

Anthropic is the second highest AI model by revenue behind ChatGPT, and they are known for using Amazon Trainium hardware and Google TPUs. However, they entered into partnerships with Nvidia and Microsoft (MSFT) on November 18, and this indicates Google and Amazon have limited scope in terms of competing with Nvidia. In the fiscal Q3 '26 call, CEO Huang said this is the first time Anthropic will be on Nvidia’s architecture.

Valuation

Ostensibly there is a valuation risk right now if customers determine they have been overspending on AI hardware and they cut back. I believe we are in the early innings at this time, and the overspending issues will not materialize until we are further along in the AI journey. The circular deals between companies in this space are additional valuation risks, and they bother me. Even if Nvidia’s investment in CoreWeave (CRWV) turns out well, I would have preferred to see them using that capital for more stock repurchases. I think the circular deals make things more cyclical than they need to be, and when we get to a situation of overspending in a few years, then the volatility may be worse than necessary.

Another valuation risk is if customers are being too aggressive with the way they depreciate GPUs. This would mean the operating income of customers is inflated, and they would have to think about lowering their GPU spend. It’s possible this is happening with other AI hardware, but I don’t think this is happening with Nvidia GPUs because CUDA is incredibly robust and it is highly backward compatible.

Here is what CEO Colette Kress said about CUDA and depreciation in the fiscal Q3 '26 call:

Most accelerators without CUDA and NVIDIA's time-tested and versatile architecture became obsolete within a few years as model technologies evolved. Thanks to CUDA, the A100 GPUs we shipped 6 years ago are still running at full utilization today, powered by [a] vastly improved software stack.

Here are some of the past numbers I look at when thinking about valuation ($ in billions):

As Nvidia employees get better at using AI to be more productive, we see the stock-based compensation (“SBC”) drop on a relative basis. It fell from 10.2% of revenue in fiscal 1Q24 down to 2.9% in fiscal Q3 '26. This is important because I think about SBC as a cash expense when looking at my adjusted FCF figure. In other words, my adjusted FCF figure for fiscal Q3 '26 is $20.4 billion, or the $22.1 billion accounting figure less $1.7 billion. This adjustment isn’t nearly as detrimental to accounting FCF as it was in fiscal Q1 '24.

Per the fiscal Q3 '26 release, management expects revenue of $65 billion for fiscal Q4 '26, with a gross margin of about 74.8% and operating expenses of $6.7 billion vs. $5.8 billion in fiscal Q3 '26. This means fiscal Q4 '26 operating income should be about $41.9 billion, or ($65 billion)*0.748 - $6.7 billion. Annualized, this comes to an operating income of $167.6 billion.

For the foreseeable future, I think Nvidia will continue adding about $5 billion of revenue per quarter, which is about $50 billion of revenue per year. Given the growth prospects, I think a reasonable valuation multiple is 30 to 35x the annualized operating income figure of $167.6 billion, which gives us a valuation range of $5.0 to $5.9 trillion.

The Q3 '26 10-Q shows 24.3 billion shares outstanding as of November 14. Multiplying by the November 20 share price of $180.64 gives us a market cap of $4.4 trillion. The market cap is below my valuation range, and I think the stock is a buy for long-term investors who plan on holding it for at least 3 years.
